perturbation.original_text: You will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials"). You recognize and acknowledge the confidential and non-public nature of the Evaluation Materials, the competitive value of the Evaluation Materials, and the damage that could result to the Company if the Evaluation Materials were used or disclosed except as authorized by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: You will endeavor to treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials"). You recognize and acknowledge the confidential and non-public nature of the Evaluation Materials, the competitive value of the Evaluation Materials, and the potential damage that could result to the Company if the Evaluation Materials were used or disclosed except as authorized by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will treat confidentially' with 'will endeavor to treat confidentially' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, making it less definitive and creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification replaces 'will treat' with 'will endeavor to treat,' creating ambiguity around the obligation to maintain confidentiality, while the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires a clear duty to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets to successfully claim misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification replaces 'will treat' with 'will endeavor to treat,' introducing ambiguity regarding the obligation to maintain confidentiality. This directly contradicts the requirement for a clear duty to maintain secrecy, as the change weakens the obligation, making it less definitive and potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section 3426.1(a), defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The original text establishes a clear duty, while the changed text introduces ambiguity, potentially hindering the ability to claim misappropriation under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by undermining the clear duty needed for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: You will not use any of the Evaluation Materials for any purpose other than the exclusive purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction. You and your representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may only be disclosed to ----------------- those of your directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "your representatives") who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company (it being understood that your representatives shall be informed by you of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by you, and shall each expressly agree, to treat such information confidentially in accordance with this Agreement), (ii) you may disclose Evaluation Materials that you have been advised by counsel are required to be disclosed under applicable securities laws; and (iii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: You will not use any of the Evaluation Materials for any purpose other than the general purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction. You and your representatives will attempt to keep the Evaluation Materials confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may only be disclosed to those of your directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "your representatives") who reasonably need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company (it being understood that your representatives should be informed by you of the confidential nature of such information and should be directed by you, and should each agree, to treat such information confidentially), (ii) you may disclose Evaluation Materials that you have been advised by counsel are required to be disclosed under applicable securities laws; and (iii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'exclusive purpose' to 'general purpose', 'will keep...completely confidential' to 'will attempt to keep confidential', 'need to know' to 'reasonably need to know', and 'shall be informed/directed' to 'should be informed/directed' introduces ambiguity and weakens the legally binding nature of the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes introduce ambiguous language that weakens the commitment to confidentiality. Cal. Civ. Code § 1644 states that words are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense. The modifications undermine the clarity and certainty of the contract, rendering the confidentiality provisions less enforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the confidentiality obligations. The original contract used 'exclusive purpose', 'will keep...completely confidential', 'need to know', and 'shall be informed/directed'. The changed text modifies these to 'general purpose', 'will attempt to keep confidential', 'reasonably need to know', and 'should be informed/directed', respectively. Cal. Civ. Code § 1644 states that words are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense. The change from 'shall' to 'should' weakens the directive, and the introduction of 'attempt' and 'reasonably' makes the obligations less definite and enforceable. The change directly contradicts the need for clear and unambiguous contractual terms.

perturbation.original_text: Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, in the event that a Possible Transaction is not consummated neither you nor your representatives shall use any of the Evaluation Materials for any purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, in the event that a Possible Transaction is not consummated neither you nor your representatives shall endeavor to avoid using the Evaluation Materials for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not use' with 'shall endeavor to avoid using' significantly weakens the prohibition on using the evaluation materials after a failed transaction, making it an aspiration rather than a strict obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, the implied obligation to *not* use confidential information post-failed transaction protects trade secrets, however, the 'endeavor to avoid' language makes it more difficult to enforce this, weakening trade secret protection, creating a contradiction when related to confidential materials that would otherwise constitute trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

CONTRACTS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE SECTION 16600-16607 16600. Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. 16601. Any person who sells the goodwill of a business, or any owner of a business entity selling or otherwise disposing of all of his or her ownership interest in the business entity, or any owner of a business entity that sells (a) all or substantially all of its operating assets together with the goodwill of the business entity, (b) all or substantially all of the operating assets of a division or a subsidiary of the business entity together with the goodwill of that division or subsidiary, or (c) all of the ownership interest of any subsidiary, may agree with the buyer to refrain from carrying on a similar business within a specified geographic area in which the business so sold, or that of the business entity, division, or subsidiary has been carried on, so long as the buyer, or any person deriving title to the goodwill or ownership interest from the buyer, carries on a like business therein. For the purposes of this section, "business entity" means any partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), limited liability company, or corporation. For the purposes of this section, "owner of a business entity" means any partner, in the case of a business entity that is a partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), or any member, in the cas
perturbation.justification: The original text created a strict obligation to not use evaluation materials, while the changed text weakens this obligation by only requiring an 'endeavor to avoid using' them. This weakened obligation contradicts the implied need to protect trade secrets. The LLM explanation correctly points out that this weakens trade secret protection, which is especially important when related to confidential materials. Although the snippets focus on non-compete agreements, they establish that California law generally voids contracts that restrain trade. The original text's strict prohibition aligns with protecting trade secrets and confidential information, while the modified text undermines this protection, creating a contradiction. Therefore, the change makes the contract significantly less enforceable in protecting legitimate business interests.
